articles.title: Confessions of a political liveblogger: ‘I enjoy it professionally – but, as a citizen, you can think the country’s going to hell in a handcart’

В своей публикации он отмечает, что, несмотря на профессиональное удовлетворение, наблюдая за британской политикой, как гражданин он испытывает тревогу из-за ухудшающегося состояния страны. Недавняя публикация "файлов Мандельсона" стала одним из событий, подчеркивающих сложность и масштаб политических процессов.

In the last 15 years, scientists have discovered another possible explanation – waste disposal. In 2012 neuroscientist Maiken Nedergaard’s lab discovered that the brain has its own cleaning process, the glymphatic system, which clears away unhelpful proteins and metabolic byproducts, and only switches on at night. articles.title: Planet nine mystery deepens as new discovery challenges hidden planet theory
articles.summary: Astronomers have spent years searching for a possible hidden giant planet far beyond Neptune. Unusual orbits among distant Kuiper Belt objects have fueled the Planet Nine theory, but recent discoveries are challenging the idea by showing more stable motion than expected. If Planet Nine exists, it may be much farther away than originally thought.

articles.title: NASA updates worsening ISS leak after crew safety alert
articles.summary: NASA says a long-running air leak aboard the ISS recently worsened, leading engineers to investigate new suspected crack locations and consider a riskier repair strategy. Astronauts were temporarily moved into a safe haven as a precaution before the repair was postponed for further analysis.

articles.title: Scientists found a new Alzheimer’s trigger and a drug that stops it
articles.summary: Researchers have identified a new Alzheimer’s target and created an experimental compound that blocks a damaging process inside brain cells. In mice, the treatment slowed nerve cell loss, reduced Alzheimer’s-related changes, and even appeared to promote healthier aging.
articles.original_description: Researchers have identified a new Alzheimer’s target and created an experimental compound that blocks a damaging process inside brain cells. articles.title: Scientists discover the brain chemical that helps you break bad habits
articles.summary: Ученые обнаружили, что нейромедиатор ацетилхолин играет ключевую роль в преодолении вредных привычек и адаптации к новым обстоятельствам. Эксперименты на мышах показали, что разочарование, вызванное неполучением ожидаемой награды, стимулирует выделение ацетилхолина, способствуя поиску новых стратегий. Блокирование ацетилхолина, напротив, приводило к укоренению старых, неэффективных привычек.
articles.original_description: Scientists have uncovered a key brain signal that helps us break old habits and adapt when circumstances suddenly change. By watching mice navigate a virtual maze, researchers found that disappointment—when an expected reward failed to appear—triggered a surge of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine, making the animals more likely to try a new strategy. When acetylcholine was blocked, the mice became less flexible and were more likely to stick with outdated choices.

articles.title: What is space-time? A mystery at the heart of reality
articles.summary: What if our biggest idea about reality is built on a hidden misunderstanding? A new philosophical look at space-time challenges the popular view that the past, present, and future all exist together in a timeless "block universe." The argument suggests that physicists may be blurring the difference between things that exist and things that merely occur, creating deep confusion about what space-time actually is.

articles.title: Scientists may have debunked one of humanity's oldest habits
articles.summary: Ancient grooves on human teeth, once hailed as evidence of tooth-picking, may simply be the result of natural wear, according to a new study of wild primates. The research also revealed that a common modern dental defect appears to be uniquely human, hinting that today's lifestyles may be reshaping our teeth in unexpected ways.

articles.title: Dementia risk linked to nitrate in drinking water, study finds
articles.summary: A major long-term study of more than 54,000 adults found that where nitrate comes from may matter far more than how much you consume. People who got more nitrate from vegetables—roughly the amount in a cup of baby spinach a day—had a lower risk of developing dementia, while higher nitrate and nitrite intake from red meat, processed meat, and even drinking water was linked to a greater risk.

articles.title: South Australia’s koala boom could end in mass starvation
articles.summary: В Южной Австралии наблюдается резкий рост популяции коал, который может привести к их массовому голоду из-за неспособности лесов обеспечить достаточное количество пищи. Исследователи предлагают целенаправленное снижение рождаемости как способ предотвратить голод и разрушение среды обитания.
articles.original_description: South Australia’s koala population has grown so large that it may be heading toward a self-made disaster, with forests struggling to support the animals. Researchers say targeted fertility control could prevent widespread starvation and habitat collapse before it’s too late.

articles.title: Everyone thought these helmets were Roman until scientists uncovered the truth
articles.summary: Исследователи установили, что 43 шлема, обнаруженные у берегов Испании, на самом деле относятся к Средневековью, а не к римской эпохе, развеивая многолетнюю теорию.  Это открытие проливает свет на существовавшую в Средиземноморье сеть военных поставок и торговли во время пиратства и войн.
articles.original_description: Researchers have solved a decades-old mystery by showing that a cache of 43 helmets found off the Spanish coast is medieval, not Roman. The remarkable discovery exposes a thriving weapons trade network that connected Mediterranean powers during a time of piracy, warfare, and growing demand for military equipment.

articles.title: Doctors thought this kidney drug helped some patients. It may help millions more.
articles.summary: Новое исследование показало, что препарат финаренон может оказывать защитное действие на почки и сердце у гораздо большего числа пациентов, чем считалось ранее.  Клинические испытания выявили значительное замедление прогрессирования заболеваний почек и снижение рисков сердечно-сосудистых осложнений, как у пациентов с диабетом, так и с заболеваниями почек недиабетического генеза.
articles.original_description: A trio of major studies found that finerenone may protect the kidneys and heart in far more people than previously thought. The drug significantly slowed kidney disease progression and reduced the risks of kidney failure, heart failure, cardiovascular death, and overall mortality. Researchers saw benefits not only in patients with diabetes but also in those with non-diabetic kidney disease, a group with limited treatment options.

articles.title: Heat breaks the rules at the nanoscale and scientists used it to their advantage
articles.summary: Scientists used nanoscale gold metamaterials to supercharge heat transfer across tiny gaps, achieving up to four times more energy flow than similar conventional systems. The breakthrough could lead to better chip cooling, more efficient energy technologies, and a new era of precision heat engineering.

articles.title: Scientists found a surprisingly simple way to create powerful quantum states
articles.summary: A team at the University of Chicago has discovered a surprisingly simple way to create powerful quantum states that are normally difficult to produce. By making small adjustments to the energy levels of atoms inside an optical cavity, researchers can generate a wide variety of highly entangled states without adding complicated hardware.

articles.title: Tiny X-ray telescope could unlock the Moon's hidden chemistry
articles.summary: A lightweight new X-ray telescope could finally give scientists something they’ve never had before: a complete chemical map of the Moon. Researchers used detailed mission simulations to show that a compact telescope orbiting the Moon could identify key elements across the entire lunar surface, helping reveal how the Moon formed and evolved.

articles.title: Ancient Chinese medicine could transform hair loss treatment
articles.summary: A traditional Chinese medicinal root used for over a thousand years is attracting new scientific attention for its potential to combat hair loss. Studies suggest Polygonum multiflorum can block harmful hormones, activate hair-growth signals, protect follicles, and boost blood flow to the scalp. Researchers say the herb’s effects align remarkably well with both ancient descriptions and modern hair biology.

articles.title: Scientists finally complete Schrödinger’s 100-year-old color theory
articles.summary: Researchers have finally resolved a key problem in a 100-year-old theory of color, showing that the qualities we perceive in colors are intrinsic to the mathematics of color space itself. The discovery sharpens our understanding of human vision and could lead to more precise color technologies and visualizations.

articles.title: Scientists sound the alarm as dangerous amoebas spread globally
articles.summary: Scientists warn that free-living amoebae may be an underappreciated public health threat, capable of causing deadly infections and shielding other dangerous microbes from water treatment. Climate change and aging infrastructure could help these resilient organisms spread more widely in the years ahead.

articles.title: Giant fire tornadoes could clean up oil spills faster with less pollution
articles.summary: Researchers have shown that controlled fire whirls can clean up oil spills faster and more cleanly than traditional burning methods. The spinning flames consumed up to 95% of the oil, cut soot emissions by 40%, and could help prevent spills from reaching sensitive marine habitats.

articles.title: Hidden supermassive black hole pairs may finally have a visible signal
articles.summary: Ученые предложили новый метод обнаружения пар сверхмассивных черных дыр, заключающийся в поиске звезд, чье свечение периодически усиливается из-за гравитационного линзирования черными дырами. Анализ времени и яркости этих вспышек может предоставить уникальную информацию о черных дырах, медленно приближающихся друг к другу и готовящихся к столкновению.
articles.original_description: Scientists have proposed a new method for finding tightly bound supermassive black hole pairs by searching for stars that flash repeatedly as their light is magnified by the black holes’ gravity. The timing and brightness of these bursts could provide a unique fingerprint of black holes slowly spiraling toward a future collision.

articles.title: Scientists discover why ozempic may not work for some people
articles.summary: Scientists have identified genetic variants that may make some people less responsive to GLP-1 drugs used to treat Type 2 diabetes. Roughly 10% of the population carries these variants, which appear to cause a mysterious form of "GLP-1 resistance." In several clinical trials, carriers were significantly less likely to reach healthy blood sugar targets while taking GLP-1 medications.

articles.summary: A team at the University of Minnesota discovered that changing a metal film's thickness by just a few nanometers can dramatically alter how it behaves electronically. The finding reveals a surprising new way to control metals and could help power future advances in electronics, catalysis, and quantum technology.

articles.title: Poor sleep linked to rising cancer risk in under-50s
articles.summary: Новые исследования связывают плохое качество сна с ростом риска развития рака у людей младше 50 лет.  По данным двух крупных исследований, наблюдается значительный рост числа случаев рака у молодых людей за последние десятилетия, что может быть связано с недостаточным сном. Эта информация дополняет усилия по объяснению увеличения заболеваемости раком среди молодого поколения по всему миру.
articles.original_description:
Findings add to growing efforts to explain why cancer rates are increasing among younger adults worldwide

Poor sleep may be fuelling the global rise in under-50s being diagnosed with cancer, two large studies suggest.

The number of younger people diagnosed with the disease has risen by almost 80% in three decades. Worldwide cases of early-onset cancer increased from 1.82m in 1990 to 3.26m in 2019, while cancer deaths among people in their 40s, 30s or younger rose by 27%.

articles.title: James Blood Ulmer, adventurous US guitarist and vocalist, dies aged 86
articles.summary:
Musician who spliced jazz, funk and blues, including in a spell on a major label in the early 1980s, was celebrated as ‘fearless’ by his family

James Blood Ulmer, the US guitarist celebrated for his avant garde splicing of jazz, blues and funk, has died aged 86.

A statement on social media said he died on 3 June. “His music was fearless, and so was his spirit,” his family added in another statement.

articles.title: Teenage sensation Gout Gout ready to dive in at Diamond League deep end
articles.summary:
Young sprinter up against the ‘big boys’ for the first time but he still takes life in the spotlight in his stride

Life comes at you fast, especially when you are Gout Gout. In April, the 18-year-old prodigy became the fastest teenager over 200m in history. Then last month, he finally got his own bedroom for the first time, having bought his family a new six-bedroom house in Brisbane. Now, in Oslo on Wednesday, he is one of the headline acts in his first senior Diamond League race.
